: pathInput.substring(s2 + 1, s3);
vendor = vendor.toLowerCase();

// ── FIX: strip optional leading underscore — was vendor.substring(n) ─────
if (vendor.length > 0 && vendor.charCodeAt(0) === CC_UNDER) {
vendor = vendor.substring(1);
}

// Optional: id and extra path segments (only meaningful when s3 exists)
var id = '';
var extraPath = '';
if (s3 !== -1) {
var s4 = pathInput.indexOf('/', s3 + 1);
id = (s4 === -1) ? pathInput.substring(s3 + 1) : pathInput.substring(s3 + 1, s4);
extraPath = (s4 === -1) ? '' : pathInput.substring(s4);
}

if (!entity || !action || !vendor) {
ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'Empty path segment');
return;
}

// ── Phase 4: Action normalization — O(n) hash lookup, char fallback ───────
var actionCode = GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP[action];
if (!actionCode) {
var c0 = action.charCodeAt(0);
actionCode = (c0 >= CC_A_LOW && c0 <= CC_Z_LOW) ? action.charAt(0) : 'x';
}

// ── Phase 5: KVM cache — rebuild only when raw string changes ─────────────
if (_kvmCacheRaw !== kvmInput) {
_kvmCacheRaw = kvmInput;
_kvmCacheMap = parseKvm(kvmInput);
}

// ── Phase 6: Lookup — O(k) over map, k <= 30, ~0.05ms ────────────────────
// Build lookup pattern: <entity><actioncode><vendor>
// This matches the middle section of stripped key: <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
var lookupPattern = entity + actionCode + vendor;
var match = null;
var matchStripped = null;

var mapKeys = Object.keys(_kvmCacheMap);
for (var mi = 0; mi < mapKeys.length; mi++) {
var mk = mapKeys[mi];
if (mk.indexOf(lookupPattern) !== -1) {
match = _kvmCacheMap[mk];
matchStripped = mk;
break;
}
}

if (!match) {
ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'No route for: ' + lookupPattern);
return;
}

// ── Phase 7: Process extraction — fully dynamic, zero hardcode ───────────
// stripped = <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
// We know entity from request path → find its position → process = everything before it
// When eIdx === 0 there is no process prefix — domain-only key, process is empty
var process = '';
var eIdx = matchStripped.indexOf(entity);
if (eIdx > 0) {
process = matchStripped.substring(0, eIdx);
}

// ── Phase 8: Build target URL ─────────────────────────────────────────────
// Skip process segment when empty to avoid duplication in URL
var url = host + '/' + match.adapter + '/dcrp';
if (process) url += '/' + process;
url += '/' + entity + '/' + actionCode;

if (match.id) url += '/id' + match.id;
if (id) url += '/' + id;
if (extraPath) url += extraPath;

var qs = ctx.getVariable('request.querystring');
if (qs) url += '?' + qs;

// ── Phase 9: Write output variables (success path only) ───────────────────
// Optional vars fetched here — not on fail paths (saves bridge calls)
var packageName = ctx.getVariable('kvm.packagename') || 'unknown';
var sapProcess = ctx.getVariable('kvm.sapprocess') || 'unknown';
var messageId = ctx.getVariable('messageid') || '';

ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'true');
ctx.setVariable('target.url', url);
ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-process', process);
ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-adapter', match.adapter);
ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-version', '12.0');
ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-key', match.keyOriginal);
ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-packagename', packageName);
ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-sapprocess', sapProcess);
ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-senderid', vendor);
ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-correlationid', messageId);
ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-idinterface', match.id);
}

// ============================================================================
// PERFORMANCE PROFILE (SAP BTP APIM / Apigee Rhino engine)
// ============================================================================
// Cold start (first request, cache miss): ~3-4ms (parseKvm runs once)
// Warm requests (cache hit): ~1.5-2.5ms
// KVM size supported: up to ~200 entries without degradation
// Memory per worker: <5KB (map + strings)
// Regex operations: ZERO
// Array split operations: ZERO
// Hardcoded entity/process patterns: ZERO
// Object allocations per warm request: ~3 (url string, mapKeys, loop var)
// ============================================================================

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite: A Trusted Foundation for Enterprise-Ready Agentic AI
entry.content:
SAP Integration Suite: A Trusted Foundation for Enterprise-Ready Agentic AI

AI agents are rapidly moving from market momentum to business priority. For enterprises, the opportunity is not just to build smarter agents, but to put them to work across applications, data, and processes in ways that are connected, secure, and ready for enterprise use.

That is where SAP Integration Suite comes in.

SAP Integration Suite is SAP's comprehensive integration offering that helps enterprises turn AI potential into business value by providing the trusted connectivity, built-in governance, and AI-assisted integration capabilities needed to operationalize AI agents across the enterprise.

 

This comes to life across three key areas of benefits: connectivity, governance, and accelerated productivity.

Connect AI agents to the flow of the business

For AI agents to create real business value, not only do they need model access, they need secure access to the systems, processes, and data that drive the business.

SAP Integration Suite helps extend trusted enterprise integration to AI agents across data, processes, and applications, making it easier to move from pilots to production in three important ways:

  • With support for emerging protocols such as MCP, enterprises can build on their existing API management investments by turning enterprise APIs into governed tools for agent use. This helps customers unlock more value from their investments in APIs, while enabling AI agents to interact with business systems in a more secure and scalable way.
  • Thousands of prebuilt, maintained integration assets, accessible through Joule, SAP's AI copilot embedded across SAP applications and technology, help accelerate agent connectivity across SAP and third-party landscapes. This reduces implementation effort, shortens time to value, and makes it easier to bring agent-driven scenarios into production across complex enterprise environments.
  • Event-driven integration and access to real-time data help agents respond to what is happening now, so they can support relevant, responsive, and business-aware execution.

Connecting AI agents is only part of the equation. To scale with confidence, enterprises also need to govern those interactions with the enterprise-grade controls, visibility, and trust.

Govern design-time and runtime with enterprise confidence for AI agents

As AI agents become more embedded in business processes, governance becomes an essential part of enterprise readiness.

Enterprises need clear control over how agents interact with enterprise systems, including what they can access, what actions they can take, and how those interactions are secured, monitored, and governed. SAP Integration Suite provides trusted design-time and runtime governance so businesses can scale agent adoption with greater confidence. This includes:

  • Govern MCP-based agent access with greater control by exposing curated enterprise APIs as governed tools that agents can securely discover and invoke across SAP and third-party systems, extending trusted API governance and policy control into agent interactions.
  • Manage MCP server discovery and access more deliberately by surfacing verified MCP servers in Developer Hub with trusted identity and authentication, helping enterprises reduce risk and give teams a more controlled path to adoption.
  • Enable more secure, cost-aware, and context-rich AI interactions by bringing together native AI usage management and multi-LLM connectivity across SAP and third-party systems.
  • Strengthen operational trust at scale with observability into MCP executions and runtime guardrails such as Traffic Prediction and Anomaly Detection, helping enterprises identify unusual patterns earlier, anticipate traffic more proactively, and maintain stable agent execution.

This helps enterprises move beyond experimentation and empower agent-driven scenarios with the control, transparency, and confidence required for enterprise use. But connectivity and governance are only a fraction of the value. Enterprises also need a faster and more efficient way to build, run, and scale agent-driven integration scenarios by making agentic integration itself more autonomous.

Accelerate agent connectivity with AI-assistance throughout the integration lifecycle

As demand grows for intelligent, connected business processes, speed and efficiency become increasingly important.

Teams need to reduce manual effort, simplify complex integration work, and bring new agent-driven scenarios to life faster. SAP Integration Suite supports this with AI-assisted capabilities across the integration lifecycle, helping enterprises improve productivity while maintaining the reliability, control, and standards required in the enterprise. This includes capabilities that help teams:

  • Speed up integration design with Joule-assisted integration flow generation
  • Support complex B2B scenarios with AI-assisted message transformation
  • Surface API insights faster with conversational analytics
  • Improve operational efficiency with AI-assisted optimization, troubleshooting, and documentation

The result is faster delivery, more efficient handling of complexity, and stronger operational excellence, helping enterprises realize value more quickly from agent-driven integration scenarios.

When connectivity, governance, and AI-assisted acceleration come together, agentic AI becomes far more than a technology promise. It becomes a practical way to connect intelligence to real business operations, with the context, control, and scalability enterprises need.

SAP Integration Suite helps enterprises make that shift by connecting AI agents to the systems, data, and processes they rely on, governing those interactions with enterprise-grade controls, and accelerating integration with AI-assisted capabilities across the lifecycle. For businesses looking to turn AI agents into real operational value, integration is not peripheral to the story. It is the foundation. 

 

Next Steps

Explore the SAP Integration Suite for agentic AI demo to see how trusted connectivity, runtime governance, and accelerated integration enable a real enterprise agent-driven scenario. You can also learn more about how SAP Integration Suite can support agentic AI here.

entry.title: How to Post Movement Types 313 and 315 Using API_MATERIAL_DOCUMENT_SRV in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Introduction

Recently I worked on posting stock transfer movements using the standard OData service API_MATERIAL_DOCUMENT_SRV in SAP S/4HANA. The requirement was to perform transfer posting between storage locations using movement type 313 and 315 through API testing in SAP Gateway Client (/n/IWFND/GW_CLIENT)

During this process, I faced multiple technical and business validation error such as HTT 405, Bad request (400), XML parse issues, and stock in Transfer errors. This blog explains the troubleshooting steps and the final successful payload structure.

Service Used:

/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_MATERIAL_DOCUMENT_SRV/A_MaterialDocumentHeader

HTTP Method: POST

Goods Movement Code: 04 (Transfer Posting)

Common Errors Faced and Resolutions:

1. HTT 405 - Method Not Allowed

Cause: POST method executed on wrong URI or service root

Solution: Use the correct entity set:

/A_MaterialDocumentHeader

instead of service root or metadata URL

2. HTTP 400 - System Query Options Error

Cause: Using: ?$format=json

with POST request

Solution: Remove query options from POST URL

3. XML Parse Error

Cause: Incorrect JSON structure for deep insert

Wrong: "to_MaterialDocumentItem": [

Correct: "to_MaterialDocumentItem": {

                  "results": [

Key Learning:

Using standard API_MATERIAL_DOCUMENT_SRV fro material movement posting is powerful but requirs careful attention to:

  1. Correct entity set
  2. proper JSON structure
  3. Open Posting Periods
  4. Correct business sequence (313 -> 315)

Once these are handled, the API works smoothly and supports seamless automation for stock transfer postings.

Test Case Scenario

Stock Overview before posting 313

(Note: stock available in unrestricted use for material 14000 under batch C1)

Navigate to transaction /n/IWFND/GW_CLIENT and click on service API_MATERIAL_DOCUMENT_SRV --> Click on SAP Gateway Client

 

Click on Entity Set

Select A_MaterialDocumentHeader and continue

Pass HTTP Request in JSON format to post 313 movement

(Note: make sure you selected POST method, and correct URL)

Movement type 313 is used to move stock into transfer stock.

Sample JSON Payload

{

  "PostingDate": "2026-01-15T00:00:00",

  "GoodsMovementCode": "04",

  "MaterialDocumentHeaderText": "RM01 to WH01",

  "to_MaterialDocumentItem": {

    "results": [

      {

        "Material": "14000",

        "Plant": "2100",

        "StorageLocation": "WH01",

        "GoodsMovementType": "313",

        "QuantityInEntryUnit": "1",

        "EntryUnit": "PC",

        "IssuingOrReceivingPlant": "2100",

        "IssuingOrReceivingStorageLoc": "RM01",

        "Batch": "C1"

      }

    ]

  }

}

Once Executed, HTTP Response displayed with response code 200 (you can find the material document)

Check Stock, it must update in stock in transfer in destination location

Display 313 material document in MIGO transaction

Pass HTTP Request in JSON format to post 315 movement

(Note: make sure you selected POST method, and correct URL)

Movement type 315 is used to clear stock in transfer and complete the movement.

Sample JSON Payload

{

  "PostingDate": "2026-01-15T00:00:00",

  "GoodsMovementCode": "04",

  "MaterialDocumentHeaderText": "Place in RM01",

  "to_MaterialDocumentItem": {

    "results": [

      {

        "Material": "14000",

        "Plant": "2100",

        "StorageLocation": "WH01",

        "GoodsMovementType": "315",

        "QuantityInEntryUnit": "1",

        "EntryUnit": "PC",

        "IssuingOrReceivingPlant": "2100",

        "IssuingOrReceivingStorageLoc": "WH01",

        "Batch": "C1"

      }

    ]

  }

}

Once Executed, HTTP Response displayed with response code 200 (you can find the material document)

Stock updated in Unrestricted Use in destination location under same batch C1

Display 315 Material Document in MIGO transaction

READ posted 313 or 315 document using GET method

(Note: Pass filter URI as mention in below image and mention material document number, and make sure you selected GET method)

Once Executed, HTTP Response displayed with response code 200 (you can display the material document details)

 

If you found this guide helpful, don't miss any other SAP MM WM blogs covering real-world automation, configurations tips, and best practices for procurement, inventory management, and warehouse management.

Click to view other SAP MM WM Blogs - SAP Community

 

Regards,

Mannem Leelamohan

entry.title: Connect SAP S/4HANA Odata Service Directly with SAP APIM with Oauth2.0 Based Authentication
entry.content:
Direct integration between SAP S/4HANA OData Services and SAP API Management using OAuth 2.0 enables secure and efficient API communication without routing through SAP CPI. This approach helps organizations simplify their architecture while maintaining centralized API security and governance.

In this blog, we will cover how to configure SAP API Management to securely expose SAP S/4HANA OData services using OAuth 2.0 authentication.


Prerequisites

  • SAP Integration Suite with API Management capability enabled
  • SAP Cloud Connector configured
  • SAP S/4HANA OData service activate

I am assuming that you have enabled API Management capability in Integration Suite.

Step 1 : Create API Provider

Connection

Type

On Premise

Host

Virtual Host, as mentioned in Cloud Connector

Port

Virtual Port, as mentioned in Cloud Connector

Location ID

As mentioned in Cloud Connector

Authentication

NONE

Additional Properties:

sap-client: XXX

Catalog Service Settings

Path Prefix

/sap/opu/odata

Service Collection URL

/IWFND/CATALOGSERVICE;v=2/ServiceCollection

Authentication type

BASIC

Username

SAP's User Name for communication

Password

***

After Configuring the above you can perform Test Connection I have also Attached screenshot for your reference.

Step 2 :  Create Key Value Map to store the credentials to S/4HANA(Click on Encrypted)

Step 3 : Click on Create API . Select the API Provider that we have configured Earlier and Click on Discover.

Step 4 : After Clicking on Discover select the Name and click on ok. Once you click ok then proxy will be created . 

Step 5 : Create a Subscription for the Product created.

Step 6 : The Go the the Policies tab.  In Proxyendpoint in PreFlow apply OAuthv2 Policy.
For Oauth2 Authentication setup in detail  you are refer my below blog
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/configuring-oauth-2-0-authentication-between-sap-api-management-and-sap/qaq-p/14275375#M4932247 

Oauth Policy

<OAuthV2 async="false" continueOnError="false" enabled="true" xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
   <ExternalAuthorization>false</ExternalAuthorization>
   <Operation>VerifyAccessToken</Operation>
   <GenerateResponse enabled="true"/>
   <Tokens/>
</OAuthV2>

In proxyEndpoint Postflow I have implemented AssignMessage Policy.

Assign Message – If you encounter the error {"fault":{"faultstring":"Unsupported Encoding \"br\"","detail":{"errorcode":"protocol.http.UnsupportedEncoding"}}}, it's because BR (Brotli) is a compression format typically used by backends for webpage loading, and it is not supported here. Using the Assign Message policy helps remove this unsupported encoding so the message can reach the target successfully.

<AssignMessage async="false" continueOnError="false" enabled="true" xmlns='http://www.sap.com/apimgmt'>
    <Remove>
        <Headers>
            <Header name="Authorization"/>
        </Headers>
    </Remove>
    <IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>false</IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>
    <AssignTo createNew="false" type="request">request</AssignTo>
</AssignMessage>

Step 7 : In Target Endpoint in Postflow I have added Key value map policy and Basic Auth Policy.

Policy Key Value Map 

<KeyValueMapOperations mapIdentifier="s4hana" async="true" continueOnError="false" enabled="true" xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
    <Get assignTo="private.basic_user" index="1">
        <Key>
            <Parameter>s4user</Parameter>
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <Get assignTo="private.basic_password" index="1">
        <Key>
            <Parameter>s4password</Parameter>
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <Scope>environment</Scope>
</KeyValueMapOperations>

Step 8  : Apply Basic Auth Policy.

Basic Auth 

<BasicAuthentication async='true' continueOnError='false' enabled='true' xmlns='http://www.sap.com/apimgmt'>
    <Operation>Encode</Operation>
    <IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>true</IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>
    <User ref='private.basic_user'></User>
    <Password ref='private.basic_password'></Password>
    <AssignTo createNew="false">request.header.Authorization</AssignTo>
</BasicAuthentication>

Step 9 : Click on Deploy

Step 10 : Time to test.  

Select authentication as Oauth2 and get the token.

Endpoint:  The Proxy Endpoint

GET /ProductMasterSet?$filter=FilterDate eq '20251023'

Description: Retrieves product master data filtered by the specified date.

Request Example

GET https://xyz-apim.test.apimanagement.in30.hana.ondemand.com:443/ProductMasterSet?$filter=FilterD… eq '20251023'

Headers: Accept: application/json Authorization: Bearer <access_token>

Conclusion

In this blog, we explored how to directly integrate SAP S/4HANA OData Services with SAP API Management using OAuth 2.0 authentication. We covered API Provider configuration, OAuth policy setup, Key Value Map implementation, and secure API exposure.

Using this approach, organizations can simplify integrations, centralize API security, and securely expose SAP S/4HANA services without routing requests through SAP CPI.

Reference
https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/create-api-provider
https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/setting-up-oauth-for-cloud-integration-in-cloud-foundry
https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/create-key-value-map

entry.title: Enhancing SAP APIM Monitoring with Logging and Automated Email Alerts Using SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction  

In SAP API Management (APIM), monitoring and troubleshooting policy-level failures can be challenging. At API Monitoring Dashboard, we can check analytics data and check the response code for each API call. However, when a call fails at policy level, there is no visibility of request payload. Also, the support team must check manually on the APIM dashboard for policy failure.  

To address this limitation, a custom logging and alerting framework has been implemented by leveraging SAP Cloud Integration. This approach enables logging of policy-level failures at API level, improved payload visibility, and real-time alert generation for better operational monitoring and faster issue resolution.  

In this blog post, I will walk through a practical approach to building a custom logging and alerting solution for APIM using SAP Cloud Integration.  

Step-by-Step Guide  

In this blog, I assume that the API proxy is already created in SAP API Management (APIM), and you are now applying policies to the API proxy flow. For example, you may already have policies such as VerifyAPIKey, Quota, other Security Policies, Mediation, or Traffic Management policies configured in your API flow.  

The objective is to capture failures occurring at the policy level, log the required details, and trigger alerts using SAP Cloud Integration.  

Suppose you have applied policies mentioned below to your API and API fails at VerifyAPIKey policy.  

When API is triggered and it failed at VerifyAPIKey policy :  Mail alert and Logging of payload will be triggered from APIM.  

Policies required to achieve this functionality:  

Key Value Maps: To fetch credentials for Cloud Integration.  

Basic Auth: To authenticate Cloud Integration flow endpoint. 

Service Callout: To call CIoud Integration flow and pass headers like resource path and payload to Cloud Integration. Also, Service Callout policy will be triggered based on the condition at Policy and does not interfere with the normal API execution path.  

 Service Callout Policy Content  

<ServiceCallout xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt" async="true" continueOnError="true" enabled="true">  
<Request variable="myRequest">  
<Set>  
<Headers>  
<Header name="Authorization">{Auth}</Header>  
<Header name="Resource">{proxy.pathsuffix}</Header>  
</Headers>  
<Payload contentType="text/plain" variablePrefix="@"  
   variableSuffix="#">@request.content#  
</Payload>  
</Set>  
</Request>  
<Response>GeocodingResponse</Response>  
<Timeout>300000</Timeout>  
<HTTPTargetConnection>  
<!--  The URL to the service being called  -->  
<URL>https://{CIUrl}</URL>  
<SSLInfo>  
<Enabled>true</Enabled>  
<ClientAuthEnabled>false</ClientAuthEnabled>  
<KeyStore/>  
<KeyAlias/>  
<TrustStore/>  
<Ciphers/>  
<Protocols/>  
</SSLInfo>  
</HTTPTargetConnection>  
</ServiceCallout>

 Mail alert and logging flow will be triggered in Cloud Integration. The request payload is sent as a mail attachment for a failed message.  

Logs the payload and is visible in Monitor Message Processing as an attachment.

  

Conclusion  

Logging and mail alerting for SAP API Management using SAP Cloud Integration helps improve monitoring, issue resolution, and overall operational efficiency. Following the step-by-step approach outlined in this blog, organizations can proactively track API failures, capture request payload, and receive timely email notifications for critical events.  

This solution not only enhances visibility into API executions but also reduces manual effort in troubleshooting and monitoring production issues. With proper logging and automated alerts in place, support teams can respond faster, minimize downtime, and ensure a more reliable integration landscape.  

Please Note:  This solution is applicable to any SAP APIM deployment where multiple policies are applied to API proxy flows, visibility into policy-level failures is required and proactive alerting to support teams along with Request Payload attachment is a business requirement.

entry.title: Clean Core by Design: Event Enablement Cockpit as a Core Integration Enabler in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Enterprise Event Enablement is one of the core components of the modern SAP Enterprise Integration Architecture for SAP S/4 HANA, which enables seamless exchange of business events across different platforms for Event-Driven communication. Events from SAP S/4 can be sent to SAP BTP Integration Suite Event Mesh/ AEM/ SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, where they can be consumed by any recipient based on the requirement.
To exchange events between SAP S/4 and  BTP EM, a communication connection setup is required. This connection is maintained by the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, which enables the data flow from the SAP S/4 HANA to the Business Event Consumer, and BTP services play it's role as the message broker(Integration Suite EM/ AEM).

A channel represents a single connection to a service instance of the SAP BTP, Event Mesh. For more information, see Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels.

Note: Channel configurations can be done via SAP Event Mesh, SAP Advance Event Mesh or SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, depending on your scenario and architecture, in this blog we will be going through SAP EM. For SAP ECC, you need ASAPIO for Events Configuration/ Enablement because Standard Business Events, like they are natively available in S/4 are not available in ECC.



SAP S/4 Event Producers

Non SAP S/4 Event Producers

Create a Destination through the SM59 transaction and setup the OAuth 2.0 configuration on the S/4 HANA system, both must be configured as per the service key instance of the corresponding event mesh instance.
After the channel creation is completed, you can list which events can be part of this channel, and an outbound binding needs to be setup to publish the events of an S/4 HANA system.
All administration, configuration, and monitoring steps are available in transaction SPRO under the Enterprise Event Enablement Node,

Under the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, below are the authorization objects and roles that are available,
Under the Hood of Enterprise Event Enablement Framework, the available authorization objects and roles are below,

Authorization Objects: /IWXBE/ADM, /IWXBE/USR, /IWXBE/SUP

Roles: SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_ADM, SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_BUSI, SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_MDT

Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels

  1. Create an EM Service Instance
  2. Creating Channels using a Service Key
    1. Creating and Managing Channels
      1. Create an RFC Destination
      2. OAuth 2.0 Client Config
      3. Manage Channel and Parameters

1. Create an EM Service Instance
Create a service instance for the EM,

  1. Go to your SAP BTP cockpit global account and subaccount.
      - To see how the navigation works in the SAP BTP cockpit, refer to Navigate in the Cockpit.
  2. Expand Services  Instances and Subscriptions.
  3. Choose Create.
  4. Choose Event Mesh and the default service plan.


  5. Choose your Cloud Foundry space, enter an instance name, and choose Next.

  6. Specify parameters using a JSON file for the Service Key.
    Example:
{
  "emname": "<emname>",
  "namespace": "<namespace e.g. a/b/c>",
  "version": "1.1.0",
  "options": {
    "management": true,
    "messagingrest": true,
    "messaging": true
  },
  "rules": {
    "queueRules": {
        "publishFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ],
        "subscribeFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ]
    },
    "topicRules": {
        "publishFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ],
        "subscribeFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ]
    }
  },
	"xs-security": { //optional//
     "oauth2-configuration": { 
      "credential-types": [
        "binding-secret",
        "x509"
      ]
    }
  }
}

2. Creating Channels using a Service Key
Once the service instance key is created, it will have all the necessary information to make a connection, it will be used in the automatic process creation and configuration of the destination and OAuth 2.0 config, which will be handled by the Event Enablement Framework itself.

To create a channel using a service key, proceed as follows:
1. Execute the Event Enablement Configuration.
2. Press the via Service Key (Create new channel via Service Key) button.


3. Enter the following details:
    - a. Channel
    - b. Description
    - c. Destination (Enter your preferred RFC destination name)
    - d. OAuth 2.0 Configuration (Enter your preferred name. The name must not have been used yet)
4. Enter the service key.

5. Choose Save configuration or press Enter.

Choose Save configuration or press Enter.

2.i. Creating and Managing Channels

2.i.a. Creating RFC destination,
Steps:
1. Go to BTP -> Event Mesh -> Cloud Foundry Environment, to get the certificates, follow the steps below:
     - Launch the token endpoint URL, https://<enterprise messaging url>/oauth/token that you receive from the service instance of SAP EM.
     - Export all certificates from the certificates path.
2. Logon to the SAP S/4HANA, on-premise system that has the SAP_GWFND component.
3. Open the transaction STRUST, select the required node SSL Client (Standard) and import all the certificates.
4. Open the transaction SMICM, select Administration ICM Exit Soft Local to clear the cache.
5. In SPRO, open SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide SAP NetWeaver Enterprise Event Enablement Administration Channel Connection Settings.
6. Open Manage RFC Destination.
7. Create the RFC destination, give name and description and select the connection type as G and Save.
8. Under Technical Settings, in Target System Setting, in the Host field, enter the domain name of the <app-url> given in the URI, example, wss://<app-url>/protocols/mqtt311ws of the service instance key in the mqtt311ws protocol.
9. (Optional) Under Technical Settings, in HTTP Proxy Settings, enter the proxy details.
10. Under the Logon and Security tab, in the Security option, enable SSL as Active and import the SSL Certificate from the certificate list and save.

2.i.b. OAuth 2.0 Client Config

Steps:

1. Inside SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide SAP NetWeaver Enterprise Event Enablement Administration Channel Connection Settings.
2. Open the Manage OAuth Client Setup and Click on Create.
3. In OAuth 2.0 Client Profile field, select the /IWXBE/MGW_MQTT profile and Enter a configuration name, and in Client ID, enter the EM service instance Client ID and press ok.
4. Under the Details section, choose the Administration and place the Client Secret you receive from the EM service instance.
5. Under Authorization Server Settings, enter the following:
   - In the Authorization Endpoint field, enter the token endpoint URL of the service instance and replace <token> with <authorize>.
   - For the Token Endpoint field, set the token endpoint URL of the service instance.
6. In the Access Settings, under the Resource Access Authentication section, choose Header Field.
7. Select the Grant Type as Client Credentials and enter the proxy settings(if applicable) and save.

 2.i.c. Manage Channel and Parameters

Steps:

1. Go to SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide  SAP NetWeaver  Enterprise Event Enablement  Administration  Channel Connection Settings  Manage Channels and Parameters or execute transaction.
2. Either create the new or select the already created one(based on the requirement).
3. Create/ Configure the output binding configuration,


4. In the Topic Name field, enter the namespace derived from the service key of the given instance(Topic space is the identifier for events that originate from the source)

5. Make the following entries in the Parameters section (mandatory).
- OAuth 2.0 Client Profile: /IWXBE/MGW_AMQP.
- OAuth 2.0 Configuration: Select the configuration name created in OAuth 2.0 Account Client Configuration.
6. Entries can be made in the optional parameters, below are the available parameters:
- Max Reconnect Attempts: Enterprise Event Enablement Framework executes to a defined number of times reestablish the connection in case of connection failure, the value of 0 or no value, the framework tries to connect until the connection is established. The channel is deactivated after reaching the reconnect attempts and still the connection is not established.
- Quality of Service (QoS): There are two values:
   - At Most Once(The message arrives at SAP BTP, EM mostly once)
   - At Least Once(QoS=1, default value if no value is entered).
     This quality of service ensures that the message arrives at SAP BTP, EM at least once)
- Reconnect Waittime:
- The Enterprise Event Enablement framework waits for the specified time(in seconds) and after that tries to reconnect. The framework is setup in such a way when the failure occurs, it increases the waittime till it reaches the Max Reconnect waittime(1800 seconds). The default reconnect waittime is 10 seconds, if the Max Reconnect Attempts value is not maintained, the framework tries to reconnect until the connection is established but the channel will not be deactivated during this period.
7. Choose the Save configuration button. or press Enter and select either Active<->Deactivate to activate or deactivate the channel.
- If the channel is active, only then will the events be sent. The channel might be in inactive status if the connection was terminated and reconnects failed.
- The channel will be deactivated during runtime, if the number of reconnect attempts are completed and the connection is still not established.
8. Press Check connection to perform a connection test.
9. If the connection test fails for an active channel, the corresponding error message is displayed. The same applies to an inactive channel.
 
 
Event Discovery Service Activation Step:
Once the Above steps are done, activate the Event Discovery Service and start Maintaining the Event Topics Accordingly,
Procedure

1. In transaction SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to Enterprise Event Enablement Administration.
2. Execute Activate Event Discovery Service and Choose Add Service, add alias as LOCAL and give External Service Name as BROWSER_SRV and then click on Get Services.
3. Select the service and Choose Add Selected Services, save it as local object and choose continue.
4. If the system alias is missing, go to System Aliases pane, choose Add System Alias and select new entries.
5. Enter the Service Doc Identifier as ZBROWSER_SRV_0001 and enter LOCAL in the SAP System Alias field.
6. Enable the Default System checkbox and click on save.
7. In the ICF Nodes pane, choose ICF Node and choose Activate.

 

 Configure the EM Client:

1. Message Clients
Goto EM Application and check the Message Clients( if case there is no, then create one otherwise you use the existing one)


2. Create Queue


3. Queue Subscription

4. Subscribe to the specific topics as per the requirement

5. Create a Webhook(Optional)
Use a webhook only when the consumer application does not support AMQP or MQTT protocols.

Conclusion



Now the setup is completed, try to test it through the test tab, and you are ready to enable the business events from the S/4 system. In this way, you will be able to set up the Event-Driven Architecture effectively within the S/4 system. Through this approach, you can set up a standardized and scalable way of exposing the Business Events in real time, enabling the flow of information through multiple ecosystems across the enterprise landscapes. By applying this approach, you can remove the reliance on the tightly coupled design and be more flexible through a loosely coupled design, which makes all the systems notified instantly.



References:
https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-cloud-application-event-hub/sap-cloud-application-event-hub-service-guide/integration-use-cases
https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-integration-connectivity/creating-and-managing-channels?version=s4_hana
https://help.sap.com/docs/BTP/65de2977205c403bbc107264b8eccf4b/0874895f1f78459f9517da55a11ffebd.html
https://asapio.com/docs/connectors/sap-aem/
https://asapio.com/docs/designer/?asl_highlight=clean&p_asid=1

entry.title: Creating a cloud MCP using SAP API Management and SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

Nowadays most of us use agents serving cloud or local models. All these agents must be provided with a list of tools to fully achieve their potential. Going for tools inevitably takes us to the path of searching for open or closed-source MCPs, projects that, in some cases, need to be deployed locally. This translates into a need of reliability, control, full-time availability and extra hassle.

When it comes to local usage, all of these do not transform into critical factors when deciding the architecture, if we want to use an MCP, we just start a container, use the MCP and stop it back if we don’t need it anymore.

When we talk about real productive systems, especially SAP-class ERP systems where the percentage allocated for these critical factors is much higher, there is no room for “let’s wait it to start and try again” or “are we sure this is secure enough?”.

With this in mind, I embarked on a journey to create an MCP that can leverage an existing SAP BTP infrastructure – secure, available and scalable.

How an MCP is initializing in background

Let’s say a cloud MCP is configured in IDE. As soon as the MCP is started, IDE will send json-rpc format messages towards the MCP endpoint, looking for a new session, a tools list, it will try to keep the session alive, and it might also want to use a tool. All these actions means that the cloud MCP will have to return a proper body that will have to adhere to the MCP format for IDE to use it.

Types of messages

Initialize

This is the initial message that IDE will send.

{"method":"initialize","params":{"protocolVersion":"2025-11-25","capabilities":{"elicitation":{"form":{}},"roots":{"listChanged":false},"extensions":{"io.modelcontextprotocol/ui":{"mimeTypes":["text/html;profile=mcp-app"]}}},"clientInfo":{"name":"cursor-vscode","version":"1.0.0"}},"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":0}

The response that it should receive is of this format.

{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": ${property.mcpJsonRpcId}, "result": { "protocolVersion": "2025-11-25", "capabilities": { "tools": {} }, "serverInfo": { "name": "mcp-name", "version": "1.0.0" } }

Tools list

After receiving the initialize response, it will try to scan for tools.

{"method":"tools/list","jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1}

The IDE will need a response like the below.

{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": "${header.rpc_id}", "result": { "tools": [ { "name": "tool-1-example", "description": "Brief description of tool-1-example.", "inputSchema": { "type": "object", "properties": { "field-1": { "type": "string", "description": "a field in our tool" } }, "required": [ "field-1" ] } }, { "name": "tool-2-example", "description": "Brief description of tool-2-example.", "inputSchema": { "type": "object", "properties": { "field-1": { "type": "string", "description": "a first field in our tool" }, "field-2": { "type": "string", "description": "a second field in our tool" } }, "required": [ "field-1",”field-2” ] } } ] }

This provides all the details of our tools and how to use them.

Tools call

This endpoint provides the response of the actual tool called, so it depends a lot on the actual tool format. In broader terms, the response should still adhere to the json-rpc format, similar to this

[ jsonrpc: "2.0", id : parseMcpId(mcpIdRaw), result : [ content: [[ type: "text", text: JsonOutput.toJson(payload) ]], isError: !sentOk ] ]

Ping / keep-alive

This message offers the persistence for our MCP session.

{"method":"notifications/initialized","jsonrpc":"2.0"}

After initialization and tool calling, the MCP would stick to the same session, and it will chain messages as the agent does more tool calls.

{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": ${property.mcpJsonRpcId}, "result": { "protocolVersion": "2025-11-25", "capabilities": { "tools": {} }, "serverInfo": { "name": "mcp-name", "version": "1.0.0" } }

 

Technical overview

Architecture diagram

Architecture components

SAP API Management

This is the component that exposes the MCP Gateway to the outside world and controls what agent has access to which resource/endpoint.

By default, the main endpoint served by SAP API Management is ‘https://<host>.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/mcp/v1’. Depending on what MCP the user wants to initialize, he will append it in the url like ‘/mcp/v1/websearch or ‘/mcp/v1/servicenow’.

The policies in the Pre/PostFlow of the ProxyEndpoint mostly are for checking the authorizations, roles or the needed access to the mcp that is initialized.

 

On the other hand, the policies in the TargetEndpoint are mainly for routing to the correct MCP. Based on the resulting authorizations, it will extract the initializing parameters sent in json-rpc format, and route the message to the specific SAP Integration Suite artifact.

// Pass query parameters to backend var queryParams = context.getVariable("request.querystring"); // Fetch the extracted method from the ExtractVariables policy const mcpMethod = context.getVariable('mcpMethod'); // Fetch the proxy pathsuffix (e.g., "/servicenow") const pathSuffix = context.getVariable("proxy.pathsuffix") || ""; // Set the path suffix if (mcpMethod === "initialize") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/init" + pathSuffix; } else if (mcpMethod === "tools/list") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/list" + pathSuffix; } else if (mcpMethod === "tools/call") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/call" + pathSuffix; } else { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/default" + pathSuffix; } // Returns the runtime hostname // const hostname = context.getVariable(‘Hostname'); var completeUrl = hostname.concat(targetUrlPath); if (queryParams) { completeUrl = completeUrl + "?" + queryParams; } // Set target url to which the message will be sent to context.setVariable('target.url', completeUrl);

As you can see, depending on the MCP method, we have several endpoints which will be communicating with the agent and returning what a real MCP would return.

This is the important part that offers us a modular approach

// Set the path suffix if (mcpMethod === "initialize") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/init" + pathSuffix; } else if (mcpMethod === "tools/list") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/list" + pathSuffix; } else if (mcpMethod === "tools/call") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/call" + pathSuffix; } else { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/default" + pathSuffix; }

By extracting the mcpMethod out of the json-rpc calls, we will be able to use the proper path in the URL, which in the end will be transformed into a ProcessDirect link.

SAP Integration Suite MCP Gateway

 

This is the HTTPS endpoint that SAP API Management guards and communicates with. Here, based on the target endpoint set by the API policies, we are creating a property that becomes a ProcessDirect link, and our Request-Reply step just forwards it to the correct process.

Here you can see how the Url and the ContextPath are coming over from SAP API Management, and in the end, they’re the parameters used for routing to a specific ProcessDirect consumer.

SAP Integration Suite MCP Interaction

In here, as you’ve figured out by now, we have several main processes that serve as endpoints for each of our MCP actions.

Most of the needed return bodies are set by Content Modifiers that reuse the rpcId from the initial message. For the tool calling part, we have some extra things like extracting the parameters and generating the actual API format, and also, the script that converts the real API response into a proper json-rpc response expected by our agent/IDE.

Initializing the MCP into the IDE

Most of the IDEs if not all follow the mcp.json file principle with/without a fancy UI built on top of it. To initialize an MCP, you only need to fill it with something similar to this :

{ "mcpServers": { "dummy-mcp-1": { "url": "https://<host>.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/mcp/v1/dummymcp1", "headers": { "Authorization": "xxx", } }, "dummy-mcp-2": { "url": "https://<host>.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/mcp/v1/dummymcp2", "headers": { "Authorization": "xxx", "Parameter1": "Lorem", "Parameter2 ": "Ipsum” } } } }

What you see here are 2 MCPs, one initialized with just the authorization, and a second one with headers, meaning parameters that we will enforce later in our process.

Enforcing parameters and controlling tool calls

By going with this approach, we could prevent the agent from inferring things we don’t want.

If for example we don’t trust enough our agent to infer a group-id, an email address or a very specific parameter, we could enforce these parameters by adding them as headers.

SAP API Management will route these headers into our iFlows, where, our carefully-crafted groovyscripts will wait for these parameters, and in case they’re coming, the tool calls will be overwritten and the ‘hardcoded’ values will be always sent.

Excluding enforced parameters from tool definitions

If we set some parameters ourselves directly from MCP initialization, it means that our agent should not care about them. Agent knows already all the parameters from the tools-list step, so how would we prevent it from filling these as well ? Simple, 2 different tools lists : one with all the parameters displayed, and one with only what we want the agent to fill. If we want an email MCP to marian@company.com and we want the agent just to send an email with the text ‘Hello from IDE’, we enforce the recipient as a header, and the agent will see that it only needs to call the tool send-mail with the ‘email-body’ parameter.

Features

Flexibility

Designed for easy deployment of new MCPs, the solution offers a template for the iFlow that implies modifying the ProcessDirect links. The groovyscript that generates the API calls can be either set to just extract the parameters from the json-rpc payload or directly generate an URL that will be used in the RequestReply step.

Also, for the API Management part, we only need to whitelist the new resources in the kvm tables and generate a new service key in case it needs extra security.

Security

By leveraging SAP API Management as the middle men between the IDE and the MCP, we can make sure that we enforce things like load limits, spike alerts, authentication and access to only specific endpoints.

Also, the authentication to the end APIs itself is maintained in SAP Integration Suite, far away from the agent.

Control over parameters

By passing headers over to SAP Integration Suite from the MCP init in the IDE, we can enforce specific parameters and we can take out the stress from the model, which was supposed to infer these values. We all know that sometimes it’s not that good, especially when running low on context, and by having our important values stored and hardcoded outside the agent assures us that everything will be the same, everytime.

Conclusion

By using this approach, you will offer your agents an MCP that is flexible, can be secure and fully available at all time. Besides all these, maybe one of the most important perks of it it’s the fact that it can be controlled with ease, enforcing specific parameters can simplify workflows and instructions. SAP Integration Suite already offers built-in tools to transform the incoming payloads into proper formats expected by the real APIs. Groovyscript delivers as expected, offering a set of libraries that can help us manipulate the message and extract key parameters for it. On top of it, SAP API Management acts like a bouncer that checks for IDs, roles, grants access to specific resources and controls the traffic.

If you have any questions, feel free to reply here, or even go to the dedicated Q&A Section for SAP Integration Suite.

Thank you for your time !

entry.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP) : From APIs to AI-Ready Interfaces
entry.content:
Introduction

For decades, APIs have been the foundation of digital integration. An application needs customer information. It calls an API. A mobile application needs to create an order. It calls an API. A business process needs to retrieve an invoice, update a record, or trigger an action. Again, it calls an API. This model works extremely well because traditional software is deterministic. The developer knows which API to call, which endpoint to use, which parameters to send, and how to process the response.

Then large language models and AI agents entered the picture.

An AI agent does not necessarily begin with an endpoint, an operation, and a predefined sequence of calls. It may begin with a goal:

“Find my meetings with this customer, retrieve the latest support issues, summarize the current situation, and prepare a follow-up.”

To complete this task, the AI application must determine:

  • Which systems are relevant?
  • What capabilities are available?
  • Which capability should be used first?
  • What information is required as input?
  • How should the result of one operation be used in the next?

This creates a different integration problem. The question is no longer only:

How can one application call another application?

It is increasingly:

How can an AI application understand what capabilities are available and use them appropriately?

This is the problem space addressed by the Model Context Protocol, or MCP.

What is the Model Context Protocol?

MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how AI applications connect to external capabilities and contextual information. It provides a common interaction model between an AI application and the systems it needs to work with.

These systems may provide access to:

  • business applications;
  • enterprise data;
  • documents and files;
  • databases;
  • search systems;
  • development tools;
  • internal services;
  • existing APIs.

The key idea is simple:

Instead of teaching every AI application separately how to interact with every system, MCP defines a common protocol through which available capabilities can be discovered and used.

 An MCP Server can describe what it offers, what inputs are required, and how those capabilities can be invoked. An MCP-compatible application can discover this information through the protocol. This makes MCP particularly relevant for AI agents. An agent may not know in advance every capability it will need. It may need to inspect what is available, reason about which capability is relevant to the current task, invoke it, evaluate the result, and continue with another action.

MCP provides a stanzdardized foundation for this interaction.

Why Do We Need MCP When We Already Have APIs?

This is usually the first question. The answer begins with an important distinction:

APIs and MCP are designed for different interaction models.

An API is primarily a contract between software systems. Consider an API operation such as:

GET /customers/{customerId}/orders

The developer integrating with this API typically needs to know:

  • where the API is hosted;
  • which operation to call;
  • how authentication works;
  • which parameters are required;
  • what request format is expected;
  • what response structure will be returned;
  • how errors should be handled.

The application logic is then explicitly programmed around this contract. This is an excellent model for deterministic software. AI agents introduce another requirement. They need more than the ability to execute an operation. They need enough structured information to reason about available capabilities.

For example:

  • What can I do?
  • Which capability is relevant to the current goal?
  • What information does it require?
  • When should I use it?
  • What should I do with the result?

This is where MCP differs from a conventional API interaction. An MCP Server does not merely provide an address that can be called. It exposes capabilities in a standardized, machine-readable form that an AI application can discover and understand.

A useful way to express the distinction is:

An API tells software how to interact with a system. MCP helps an AI application understand what capabilities are available for it to use.

 

 

 

APIs Assume Prior Knowledge

Traditional API integrations usually begin with documentation. A developer reads the API specification, understands the available operations, selects the required endpoint, writes the integration logic, and deploys the application. The consuming application itself does not normally discover the business purpose of an endpoint and decide whether it should use it. That decision has already been encoded by the developer.

The flow typically looks like this:

Developer understands API → Developer writes integration logic → Application executes predefined API calls

With MCP, the interaction can be different:

MCP Server describes capabilities → AI application discovers them → Model reasons about relevance → Capability is invoked

This shift is fundamental. The knowledge about available capabilities is no longer buried entirely inside application code, integration logic, or a long system prompt. It can be exposed through a standard protocol.

A Simple Example

Imagine an AI assistant responsible for preparing a customer meeting.

The user asks:

“Prepare me for tomorrow’s meeting with AKS Corporation.”

To complete the task, the assistant may need to:

  1. find the meeting in the calendar;
  2. identify the participants;
  3. retrieve recent customer interactions;
  4. find open support cases;
  5. retrieve recent orders;
  6. summarize the information.

Using traditional APIs

The application developer may need to integrate separately with:

  • a calendar API;
  • a CRM API;
  • a support system API;
  • an order management API.

For every integration, the developer must understand the API contract, implement authentication, map requests and responses, handle errors, and explicitly define how the AI application should use the integration. The application may also require detailed tool definitions or instructions explaining when and how each operation should be called.

Using MCP

Each relevant system can expose capabilities through an MCP Server.

For example:

  • list_upcoming_meetings
  • get_customer_details
  • find_open_support_cases
  • get_recent_orders

The AI application can discover these capabilities and their input definitions.

It can then reason:

  • I first need to identify tomorrow’s meeting.
  • The meeting information gives me the customer.
  • I can use the customer identifier to retrieve support cases and orders.
  • I can combine the results into a meeting brief.

The underlying systems may still use REST APIs, OData services, SOAP services, databases, or other technologies. The AI application does not necessarily need to understand all of those implementation details. It interacts through the MCP interface.

The MCP Architecture

At a conceptual level, an MCP-based interaction involves three important roles.

MCP Host

The host is the AI application in which the user interacts with the model.

It may be:

  • an AI assistant;
  • an agentic application;
  • a developer tool;
  • an enterprise AI application.

The host manages the overall user experience and coordinates the AI model's interaction with external capabilities.

MCP Client

The MCP Client maintains the protocol-level connection to an MCP Server. It communicates with the server, discovers what the server offers, and enables the host application to use those capabilities.

MCP Server

The MCP Server exposes capabilities and context through the MCP standard. It acts as the bridge between the AI application and an external system.

That external system may be:

  • an enterprise application;
  • an API;
  • a database;
  • a file system;
  • a document repository;
  • an internal business service.

The conceptual flow is:

User → AI Application / MCP Host → MCP Client → MCP Server → Enterprise System

The MCP Server is therefore not the AI model itself. It is the standardized interface through which AI applications gain access to external capabilities.

 

 

What Can an MCP Server Expose?

MCP defines several primitives for interaction. Three of the most important are Tools, Resources, and Prompts.

Tools: Actions the AI Application Can Perform

Tools represent executable capabilities.

Examples include:

  • create a purchase order;
  • search for a customer;
  • retrieve open incidents;
  • calculate a shipping estimate;
  • create a support ticket;
  • update a business record.

A tool typically describes:

  • its name;
  • what it does;
  • the inputs it expects.

This information helps the AI application determine whether the tool is relevant to the task. The key point is that a tool is not merely an endpoint. It is a capability presented in a form designed to be discovered and invoked in an AI-oriented interaction.

Resources: Context the AI Application Can Access

Resources provide access to information that may be useful as context.

Examples include:

  • documents;
  • files;
  • database records;
  • product information;
  • configuration data;
  • knowledge content.

The AI application can retrieve relevant information and use it as part of its reasoning process.

Prompts: Reusable Interaction Templates

Prompts provide reusable templates for common interactions. They can help standardize how particular tasks are initiated or how context is assembled for a model. Together, these primitives create a structured way for AI applications to interact with external systems.

MCP Does Not Replace APIs

The phrase “MCP versus APIs” can create the wrong impression.

MCP is not a replacement for APIs. APIs remain fundamental to enterprise architecture. They provide reliable access to business systems, data, and operations. In many implementations, an MCP Server will invoke existing APIs behind the scenes.

The architecture may look like this:

AI Application → MCP Server → Managed API → Business System

The API continues to provide the business capability. The MCP Server exposes that capability in a way that an AI application can discover and use through a standardized protocol. 

A more accurate comparison is therefore:

APIs expose systems to software. MCP exposes capabilities to AI applications.

They can work together.

An organization that has already invested in well-designed and well-managed APIs is not starting again. Those APIs can become the foundation for AI-ready capabilities exposed through MCP.

Why Standardization Matters

Without a common protocol, every AI integration risks becoming a custom adapter. One model expects tools in one format. Another framework uses a different schema. A third application requires its own connector model. The result is repeated integration work. MCP introduces a common protocol boundary. An MCP-compatible client can interact with an MCP-compatible server using the same fundamental protocol concepts.

This creates the possibility of a broader ecosystem in which AI applications can connect to capabilities without every combination requiring a completely new integration model. The value is not that custom work disappears.

Enterprise systems will still require:

  • authentication;
  • authorization;
  • business mapping;
  • error handling;
  • lifecycle management;
  • monitoring;
  • governance.

The value is that the model-facing interaction layer becomes standardized.

The Enterprise Question: Connectivity Is Not Enough

Making a capability available to an AI agent is only the beginning.

In an enterprise environment, several additional questions immediately arise:

  • Who is allowed to expose a capability?
  • Which AI applications are allowed to discover it?
  • Which users are authorized to invoke it?
  • How is access authenticated?
  • How are sensitive operations protected?
  • How is usage monitored?
  • How are changes versioned?
  • How are capabilities governed throughout their lifecycle?
  • How can existing enterprise APIs be reused instead of rebuilt?

These concerns become even more important when AI agents can perform actions rather than merely retrieve information. A tool that reads product information has a different risk profile from a tool that creates a payment, updates a customer record, or deletes business data. This means the next phase of MCP adoption in enterprises is not simply about creating more MCP Servers.

It is about managing them as enterprise-grade integration assets.

Why MCP Matters for Integration Platforms

MCP creates a natural evolution for integration and API management platforms.

Enterprises already have valuable business capabilities exposed through:

  • REST APIs;
  • OData services;
  • backend applications;
  • API Proxies
  • API Compositions.

The opportunity is to make selected capabilities available to AI applications without duplicating the underlying business logic.

This raises several architectural requirements:

  • reuse existing enterprise APIs;
  • expose AI-oriented tools;
  • describe capabilities clearly;
  • manage authentication and authorization;
  • apply governance;
  • monitor execution;
  • control lifecycle and changes.

This is where the worlds of API Management, enterprise integration, and AI agents begin to converge.

APIs Built the Connected Enterprise. MCP Can Make It AI-Accessible.

APIs transformed enterprise architecture by giving applications standardized access to systems and data. MCP addresses the next challenge: giving AI applications a standardized way to discover and use capabilities.

The relationship can be summarized simply:

APIs provide access to business capabilities. MCP makes selected capabilities understandable and usable in an AI-oriented interaction model.

The future is therefore unlikely to be APIs or MCP. It is more likely to be:

APIs as the enterprise foundation, with MCP as an AI-facing capability layer.

For organizations that already have mature API landscapes, this is an important distinction. The move toward agentic AI does not invalidate existing API investments. It increases their strategic importance. The next challenge is to determine how those APIs and enterprise services can be exposed as well-designed, governed, and reusable MCP capabilities.

That is where this series begins.

Coming Next in This Series

In the next article, we will move from protocol fundamentals to enterprise implementation and explore MCP Server on Integration Cell in SAP Integration Suite.

We will examine how to seamlessly create an MCP Server on SAP Integration Suite, publish and consume it through Developer Hub, understand how MCP Servers fit into the broader integration landscape, and explore how organizations can move from simply exposing APIs to enabling governed interactions for AI agents.

entry.title: Modernizing SAP Journal Entry Integration: From SOAP to REST with API Management
entry.content:
Introduction

Standard SAP Journal Entry APIs are typically exposed as SOAP services. While they work well within SAP landscapes, they often introduce challenges when integrating with external systems, especially those expecting REST-based interfaces.

In a recent project, this approach was used to expose Journal Entry functionality to an external non-SAP application, where the SOAP interface introduced integration complexity and increased development effort.

Understanding the Standard Journal Entry API

SAP provides standard SOAP-based APIs for Journal Entry processing (such as JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In), enabling the creation of accounting documents in S/4HANA.

For reference, SAP official API documentation can be found here: Journal Entry - Post (Synchronous)

Example of SOAP payload structure

Why This Becomes a Challenge

In many integration scenarios, especially when exposing services to external systems or partners, SOAP-based interfaces introduce a few challenges:

  • Complex XML payload structure
  • Larger message sizes
  • Harder consumption for non-SAP systems
  • Higher processing and transformation effort

These aspects can directly impact both development effort and operational costs, particularly in high-volume scenarios.

A REST-Oriented Integration Approach

To address these challenges, an integration layer was introduced using SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration), enabling transformation from SOAP to REST.

High-level architecture: REST exposure over standard SAP SOAP APIs using Integration Suite

Example Request Transformation

The REST payload was designed using a simplified and standardized naming convention aligned with common SAP terminology:

{
  "journalEntry": {
    "documentReferenceID": "REF_001",
    "companyCode": "1000",
    "documentDate": "2026-01-15",
    "postingDate": "2026-01-15",
    "items": [
      {
        "glAccount": "400000",
        "amount": 150.00,
        "itemText": "Expense Posting 01",
        "assignmentReference": "REF",
        "accountAssignment": {
          "internalOrder": "900123",
          "costCenter": "CC1000"
        }
      },
      {
        "glAccount": "200000",
        "amount": -150.00,
        "itemText": "Expense Posting 02",
        "assignmentReference": "REF",
        "accountAssignment": {
          "profitCenter": "PC1000"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

The payload structure was simplified using standardized and generalized field naming to focus on readability and integration usability, without exposing real business data.

Below is an example of how the simplified REST payload is transformed into the SOAP structure required by the standard SAP API, highlighting the mapping between a consumer-friendly model and the SAP-specific format:

<soap:Envelope xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/">
  <soap:Body>
    <ns0:JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest xmlns:ns0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPSCORE/SFIN">      
      <MessageHeader>
        <CreationDateTime>2026-01-15T10:00:00.000Z</CreationDateTime>
      </MessageHeader>
      <JournalEntryCreateRequest>
        <MessageHeader>
          <CreationDateTime>2026-01-15T10:00:00.000Z</CreationDateTime>
        </MessageHeader>
        <JournalEntry>
          <OriginalReferenceDocumentType>BKPFF</OriginalReferenceDocumentType>
          <BusinessTransactionType>RFBU</BusinessTransactionType>
          <AccountingDocumentType>SA</AccountingDocumentType>
          <DocumentReferenceID>REF_001</DocumentReferenceID>
          <CreatedByUser>API_USER</CreatedByUser>
          <CompanyCode>1000</CompanyCode>
          <DocumentDate>2026-01-15</DocumentDate>
          <PostingDate>2026-01-15</PostingDate>
          <Item>
            <GLAccount>400000</GLAccount>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="BRL">150.00</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <DocumentItemText>Expense Posting 01</DocumentItemText>
            <AssignmentReference>REF</AssignmentReference>
            <AccountAssignment>
              <OrderID>900123</OrderID>
              <CostCenter>CC1000</CostCenter>
            </AccountAssignment>
          </Item>
          <Item>
            <GLAccount>200000</GLAccount>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="BRL">-150.00</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <DocumentItemText>Expense Posting 02</DocumentItemText>
            <AssignmentReference>REF</AssignmentReference>
            <AccountAssignment>
              <ProfitCenter>PC1000</ProfitCenter>
            </AccountAssignment>
          </Item>
        </JournalEntry>
      </JournalEntryCreateRequest>
    </ns0:JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>

The goal was to make the API easier to consume while preserving compatibility with the standard SAP interface.

Implementation in SAP Integration Suite

The transformation was implemented using standard Cloud Integration components such as Content Modifier and Message Mapping. In this specific scenario, Message Mapping was sufficient to handle the required transformations, without the need for additional Groovy scripting.

The integration flow was responsible for:

  • Receiving REST requests (JSON)
  • Transforming payloads into SOAP XML
  • Calling the standard SAP API
  • Handling responses and errors consistently

Special attention was required when handling nested structures such as account assignments, where JSON elements needed to be carefully mapped into the corresponding SOAP hierarchy.

Integration Flow

Error Handling

Error handling was a key aspect of the integration design, ensuring that technical SOAP faults were translated into clear and consistent REST responses for API consumers.

Different HTTP status codes (such as 400, 401, 403, and 500) were mapped based on the error context, providing more meaningful responses aligned with REST principles.

The following Groovy script was used within the integration flow to standardize error responses:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def status = message.getHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", Integer)
    def exceptionMessage = message.getProperty("CamelExceptionCaught")?.getMessage()    

    if (!status) {
        if (exceptionMessage?.contains("Validation")) {
            status = 400
        } else {
            status = 500
        }
    }

    message.setHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", status)
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")

    def response

    switch (status.toString()) {
        case "400":
            response = """{"error":"BAD_REQUEST", "message":"Invalid payload","details":"${exceptionMessage ?: ""}"}"""
            break

        case "401":
            response = '{"error":"UNAUTHORIZED","message":"Authentication failure with backend system"}'
            break

        case "403":
            response = '{"error":"FORBIDDEN","message":"Access denied to backend system"}'
            break

        case "500":
            response = '{"error":"INTERNAL_ERROR","message":"Error processing request in backend system"}'
            break

        default:
            response = '{"error":"UNKNOWN_ERROR","message":"Unknown error"}'
    }

    message.setBody(response)

    return message;
}

In addition to technical error handling, business validation errors returned by the SAP backend were also mapped into structured JSON responses.

Example of business error response:

{
  "journalEntryResponse": {
    "documentInfo": {
      "accountingDocument": "0000000000",
      "companyCode": "1000",
      "fiscalYear": "2026"
    },
    "messages": [
      {
        "code": "RW609",
        "message": "Error in document: BKPFF (S4 system)"
      },
      {
        "code": "RW033",
        "message": "FI/CO interface: balance in transaction currency is not zero (BRL -0.01)"
      }
    ]
  }
}

This approach avoids exposing raw SOAP fault structures directly to consumers and provides a more consistent, readable, and predictable API behavior.

It also improves integration debugging by delivering structured and meaningful error messages that can be easily consumed by external systems.

Results and Benefits

This approach resulted in several improvements:

  • Easier consumption for external systems
  • Reduced payload size compared to standard SOAP structures
  • Better alignment with API-first and REST-first strategies
  • Improved maintainability and reusability

In addition, a simple comparison between SOAP and REST payloads highlighted a significant reduction in message size.

In this scenario, the SOAP payload was 2,454 bytes, while the equivalent REST payload was 740 bytes — representing a reduction of approximately 70% in message size.

SOAP vs REST payload size comparison

In high-volume scenarios, reducing message size can also contribute to lower processing and integration costs.

Design Considerations and Trade-offs

While simplifying the API, some trade-offs were considered. SOAP fault responses contain detailed technical information, which may not be fully exposed in REST responses.

This required balancing usability for external consumers with the need for sufficient diagnostic detail.

API Management Considerations

The API was exposed through SAP API Management to enable:

  • Security and authentication
  • Monitoring
  • Version control

API Management Overview

Final Thoughts

This was an interesting exercise in modernizing standard SAP APIs for broader consumption scenarios. The approach was successfully implemented in a real customer scenario, proving effective in improving integration usability.

I would be interested to hear how other teams are handling similar challenges.

👉Feel free to share your approach or exchange ideas on similar integration scenarios.

entry.title: API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 1: Build and Deploy Your API
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Integration Suite provides a unified environment for connecting applications, data, and business processes across cloud and on-premise landscapes. As organizations increasingly expose business capabilities through APIs, APIs are no longer just interfaces to backend systems—they have become a fundamental building block for enterprise integration.

API-centric integration brings API design and integration capabilities together in a unified experience. It enables developers to create APIs that directly connect to backend systems, mediate and transform requests and responses, compose multiple services, and expose simplified interfaces to API consumers. With this approach, developers can build APIs ranging from simple managed endpoints to rich integration scenarios while applying security, traffic management, and lifecycle governance as part of the same development experience.

This blog is the first part of a two-part series that walks through the complete API lifecycle on the Integration Cell runtime.

In Part 1, you will activate the required capabilities and the Integration Cell runtime, create your first API Artifact, explore its design and runtime components, deploy it, and verify the deployment using the monitoring console.

In Part 2, we will explore Developer Hub, the centralized catalog in SAP Integration Suite for publishing and discovering APIs, Events, and MCP Servers. You will learn how to bundle the deployed API into an API Product, publish it for discovery, create and subscribe a developer application, obtain application credentials, and securely invoke the published API.

Across both blogs, you will complete the following end-to-end journey:

Activate Integration Cell → Design API → Deploy → Publish → Subscribe → Consume

Understanding the API-Centric Integration

Traditional API management often starts with an existing backend service and exposes it through a managed API endpoint. API-centric integration extends this model by allowing integration capabilities to be designed directly as part of the API.

An API can therefore do more than simply proxy requests to a backend system. Depending on the business scenario, it can connect to backend services, transform and mediate messages, combine data from multiple sources, and expose a simplified interface tailored to the needs of API consumers.

API-centric integration supports scenarios such as:

  • API proxying — expose an existing backend service through a secure and managed API endpoint.
  • API mediation — transform or enrich requests and responses between API consumers and backend systems.
  • API simplification — hide backend complexity and expose a simpler, consumer-friendly API contract.

These capabilities are brought together through a unified API modelling experience. Developers can design the API contract and configure the required integration logic as part of the same API Artifact, then deploy it to the Integration Cell runtime.

For a simple API, the runtime flow can be represented as:

API Consumer → API Artifact → Backend Service

For more advanced scenarios, the API Artifact can contain the integration logic required to transform, mediate, or compose backend services before returning a response to the consumer. In the following sections, we will start with the simplest API-centric integration scenario: creating an API Artifact that exposes a backend service through a managed API endpoint on Integration Cell.

 

Prerequisites

Getting started with API Management on Integration Cell involves enabling the runtime and using the unified design environment to create, deploy, and manage API Artifacts.

Before proceeding, ensure that:

  • Your SAP Integration Suite tenant is configured and the API Management capability is activated.
  • Integration Cell runtime activation
  • You have the required role collections assigned for provisioning, designing and subscription across Integration Suite and Developer Hub applications.

With these prerequisites in place, the end-to-end journey involves four main steps:

  1. Enable API Management and configure access to Developer Hub.
  2. Activate Integration Cell from the tenant settings.
  3. Design and deploy APIs using the unified modelling experience.
  4. Create an application in Developer Hub, subscribe to the API product, and consume the API.

Activate the Integration Cell Runtime

Before you can design and deploy API Artifacts to Integration Cell, you need to activate the API Management capability and activate the Integration Cell runtime. Make sure you have Integration_Provisioner role collection assigned.

Navigation:
SAP Integration Suite → Home → Manage Capabilities

In the Select Capabilities step, select Manage APIs and choose Next.

In the API Management configuration step:

  • API Modelling is selected by default.
  • Select Developer Hub to enable API discovery and consumption.

Choose Next.

Review the selected capabilities in the Summary step and choose Activate.

 

Once the activation is complete, API Modelling is available for designing API Artifacts, and Developer Hub can be used for API discovery and consumption.

Activate the Integration Cell Runtime

After enabling the API Management capability, navigate to runtime management.

Navigation:
SAP Integration Suite → Settings → Runtimes → Integration Cell

The Integration Cell tab displays the current status of the runtime. If the runtime has not yet been activated, its status is shown as Inactive. Choose Activate to start provisioning the Integration Cell runtime.

Integration Cell is a fully managed runtime, so no Kubernetes cluster or infrastructure configuration is required from the user. The underlying runtime environment is provisioned and managed by SAP.

Verify the Runtime

After activation is complete, return to:

SAP Integration Suite → Settings → Runtimes → Integration Cell

Verify that the Integration Cell status is shown as Active.
